attrs.title: Array pipeline: raw data → filter → sort → map → JSX

content.text: and it returns a new array containing only items for which the predicate returned

mutates the array in place. Sorting React state directly causes subtle bugs because React may not detect the mutation. Always spread a copy first:
Use a comparator function for numbers — the default lexicographic sort produces wrong results for numbers (e.g. 10 < 9 lexicographically).
Because each method returns a new array, you can chain them in a single expression. Order matters: filter first (smaller array to sort), then sort, then map to JSX.
Sorting is O(n log n). Filtering first reduces n, making the sort faster. For small arrays this is negligible, but it is the correct mental model regardless.
For complex pipelines or when the derived array is referenced more than once in the render, assign it to a
. This is more efficient than chaining two
Stable sort and locale-aware string sort
Modern JavaScript engines implement stable sort (ES2019+). Items with the same comparator result keep their original relative order. For string columns, always use
to handle accented characters and locale-specific ordering correctly:
state so users can toggle between ascending and descending. Multiply the comparator result by
Filtering and sorting large arrays on every render can be slow. Wrap the derivation in
so it only re-runs when its dependencies change:
when you have measured a performance problem. For arrays under a few hundred items the derivation is instantaneous and memoisation adds overhead rather than saving it.
Keeping original data separate from display order

Never store the sorted or filtered array in state as a replacement for the original. Keep the raw data in state and derive the display array during render. This way you can re-sort by a different column without losing items.
Mutates state — React may not re-render
Loses original order; can't re-sort cleanly
Keep raw data in state; sort in render
Always handle the case where filtering produces an empty array. Rendering an empty
is valid but confusing. A check before the return gives useful feedback:

depends on dataset size and render frequency. Use this table as a starting point:

Debouncing reduces renders; small overhead per render is OK
